subsystem: Password-storage-scheme comparison routine for an LDAP directory server. crypt_pw_cmp() verifies a user-supplied bind password against a stored crypt(3)-style hash by re-hashing the candidate with crypt_r() and comparing it, in constant time, to the stored value. It is the {CRYPT} scheme verifier in the server's password-check path.
provenance: Identified from naming and API surface: the function name crypt_pw_cmp, the crypt_r()/struct crypt_data glibc reentrant crypt(3) interface, and the slapi_ct_memcmp() helper (the slapi_ prefix is the SLAPI plugin API of the Fedora/Red Hat 389 Directory Server, historically Netscape/iPlanet Directory Server). The header banner names the file corpus/eval/eval_0031_cwe284.c and the in-code marker /* sample: primevul_c_validation_0376_fixed */ indicates this is the patched ('fixed') variant of a PrimeVul sample. No SPDX/copyright line is present in the excerpt.
trust_boundary: userpwd is fully attacker-controlled: it is the clear-text password supplied by a remote client in an LDAP BIND request, so its length, content, and (depending on the caller) its NULL-ness are hostile. dbpwd is the stored password value read from the directory entry (the userPassword {CRYPT} attribute); it is normally administrator/DB-controlled but is also the value whose length and salt bytes drive crypt_r(), so a malformed or attacker-influenced stored value is in scope. The security decision produced here (rc == 0 means 'password matches') is an authentication gate, so any logic error is an access-control bypass, and any unhandled input is a remotely reachable crash.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The routine crypt_pw_cmp takes two const char* arguments: userpwd, the clear-text candidate password from the client, and dbpwd, the stored encoded password from the directory entry. The excerpt shows no return type on line 2 (it is truncated from a preceding line, but the body returns an int rc), so the effective signature is 'int crypt_pw_cmp(const char *userpwd, const char *dbpwd)'. Its job is to return 0 when userpwd hashes to dbpwd and non-zero otherwise, i.e. it is the comparison primitive for the {CRYPT} password-storage scheme.

Local state is established with a fail-closed default: rc = -1 means 'no match / error' unless a successful comparison overwrites it, and cp = NULL means 'no computed hash yet'. dbpwd_len caches strlen(dbpwd) — the length of the stored hash, used both as a validity gate and as the comparison length. struct crypt_data data is the reentrant scratch buffer required by crypt_r(); it is a large (~128 KB in glibc) stack object. Only data.initialized = 0 is set, which is exactly the contract crypt_r() requires on first use — crypt_r() itself fills the rest — so the otherwise-uninitialized struct is acceptable per the library API.

This is the first access-control gate. crypt_r() is invoked ONLY when the stored value has length >= 3, i.e. at least the two salt characters plus at least one further byte. The comment (lines 11-12) states the security rationale explicitly: a shorter stored value would let 'any password bind as we then only compare SALTS'. crypt_r(userpwd, dbpwd, &data) hashes the client password using dbpwd as the crypt setting string: for legacy DES that means the first two characters are the salt (line 15 comment), while for modern MCF hashes ($1$, $5$, $6$, ...) crypt_r reads the full scheme/salt prefix from dbpwd. The returned pointer cp points into the data scratch buffer and is the freshly computed encoding of userpwd under dbpwd's salt/scheme. If the guard is not taken, cp stays NULL.

This is the second, decisive gate. The comparison runs only when (a) crypt_r succeeded (cp != NULL) AND (b) the stored hash and the freshly computed hash have exactly the same length (dbpwd_len == strlen(cp)). Requiring equal lengths guarantees the subsequent memcmp spans the ENTIRE hash — including the hash body, not merely the echoed salt — which is what prevents the salt-only bypass warned about on lines 11-12. When both conditions hold, slapi_ct_memcmp(dbpwd, cp, dbpwd_len) performs a constant-time byte comparison over dbpwd_len bytes and returns 0 on full equality; that result becomes rc. Using a constant-time comparator (rather than memcmp/strcmp) avoids leaking, via response timing, how many leading bytes of the hash matched. Any failure path sets rc = -1.

The function returns rc: 0 only when the constant-time compare found full equality (authentication success), and -1 in every other case (short/invalid stored value, crypt_r failure, length mismatch, or byte mismatch). The default-deny initialization on line 4 means every un-handled path is treated as authentication failure, which is the correct fail-closed posture for an auth primitive.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the central security contract and the historical bug this 'fixed' sample addresses (authentication bypass, CWE-287/CWE-303, an access-control failure CWE-284). crypt(3) always echoes the salt as the leading bytes of its output. If either guard is weakened, the danger is a salt-only comparison: consider a stored value dbpwd consisting of only the two salt characters. crypt_r(userpwd, dbpwd) would produce cp whose first bytes are exactly those salt characters regardless of userpwd. Were the code to memcmp over just the stored length (as in the vulnerable variant), the compare would cover only the salt bytes that crypt reproduces verbatim, so it would succeed for ANY userpwd, letting any client bind as the victim. The current code blocks this two ways: the dbpwd_len >= 3 guard refuses to even call crypt_r on a too-short stored value, and the dbpwd_len == strlen(cp) equality forces the memcmp to span the full hash body, not just the salt. The invariant the implementation MUST uphold is: never compare fewer bytes than the full length of a legitimately-computed hash, and never treat a stored value that is only salt (or otherwise shorter than a real hash) as comparable. Both checks must remain; removing either re-introduces the bypass the comment on lines 11-12 warns about.

userpwd is passed directly to crypt_r() as the key with no NULL check. crypt_r() dereferences the key string; a NULL userpwd therefore causes a NULL-pointer dereference and crash (CWE-476, remote denial of service). Whether this is reachable depends on the caller: if the BIND path can deliver a NULL (as opposed to empty-string "") password to this function — e.g. an unauthenticated/anonymous or malformed bind — the crash is remotely triggerable. This is a latent finding: the dereference lives inside crypt_r (not shown), but the missing guard is here. The invariant is that userpwd is non-NULL on entry; if that cannot be guaranteed by every caller, this function should reject NULL explicitly.

strlen(dbpwd) is executed unconditionally as the very first operation, before any validation. A NULL stored value (dbpwd == NULL) — e.g. an entry with a present-but-empty/absent userPassword value that the caller forwards as NULL — dereferences a NULL pointer and crashes (CWE-476). This is latent and depends on the caller's guarantees about dbpwd, but note it is not fail-closed: unlike the auth logic, there is no default-deny for a NULL stored value, only a crash. The invariant is that dbpwd is a valid non-NULL C string; a defensive NULL check returning -1 would make the routine robust to malformed directory data.

The numeric lower bound '>= 3' is weaker than the length of any real crypt hash (a legacy DES hash is 13 characters; MCF hashes are far longer). Stored values of length 3..12 pass this gate and cause crypt_r() to run, and are rejected only later by the dbpwd_len == strlen(cp) equality on line 19. The code is therefore correct today but fragile: its safety against the salt-only bypass rests entirely on the second check, so this gate should be understood as necessary-but-not-sufficient. If a future refactor loosened or removed the length-equality check while keeping only '>= 3', short crafted stored values would again permit a partial (salt-region) comparison. Treat the two checks as a single indivisible invariant.

The whole side-channel posture of the comparison depends on slapi_ct_memcmp actually being constant-time and returning 0 only on full byte equality. If that helper (implementation not provided) short-circuits on the first differing byte, or returns a value that can be 0 for non-equal inputs, this becomes a timing oracle (CWE-208) or a comparison-logic bug. The visible caller usage is correct — full length, correct operands (stored hash vs. freshly computed hash) — so the risk is a latent contract on slapi_ct_memcmp. Confirm its constant-time property and its exact return semantics.

Cryptographic strength is inherited from the scheme encoded in dbpwd, which this function does not constrain. If the stored value is a legacy 2-character-salt DES crypt hash, crypt_r silently truncates userpwd to 8 bytes and uses a 12-bit salt, so passwords are trivially brute-forceable (CWE-916, use of a password hash with insufficient computational effort). This is not a defect of crypt_pw_cmp per se but a property of what it will faithfully compare; a directory still holding {CRYPT}/DES values is weakly protected. Additionally, crypt_r can return NULL or an unusable '*'-prefixed error string on unsupported settings — NULL is handled by the cp && check on line 19, and an error string will simply fail the length/byte comparison, so those paths are safe.

summary: This is the patched variant of an LDAP {CRYPT} password verifier and, as shown, it is correct and fail-closed: the paired guards (dbpwd_len >= 3 before hashing, and dbpwd_len == strlen(cp) full-length equality before comparing) together defeat the salt-only authentication bypass the code comment warns about, and slapi_ct_memcmp gives constant-time comparison. Residual risk is latent and defensive: no NULL checks on userpwd/dbpwd (crash / DoS, CWE-476), the safety of the design hinges on both length gates staying intact, and cryptographic strength is only as good as the stored scheme (weak for legacy DES).

reviewed_anchors.anchor: crypt_pw_cmp(const char *userpwd, const char *dbpwd) — function signature (line 2)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Authentication primitive whose inputs cross the trust boundary; userpwd is remote/attacker-controlled and neither argument is NULL-checked.
Fail-closed default-deny initialization; carries no negative security contract — it is the safe default.
Initialized to NULL and guarded by the cp && check on line 19 before use; no unguarded dereference.
Unconditional strlen on a caller-supplied pointer with no NULL check — latent NULL dereference (CWE-476).
Large (~128 KB) stack scratch buffer required by crypt_r; only a stack-footprint note, not a memory-safety or access-control defect. Only .initialized must be preset, which it is.
Correctly satisfies the crypt_r() first-use contract; leaving the rest of the struct uninitialized is permitted by the API.

Access-control gate that decides whether hashing occurs; the '>= 3' bound is weaker than any real hash length and is safe only because of the line-19 equality — worth calling out as a fragile, indivisible pair.
Passes attacker-controlled userpwd as the crypt key with no NULL check (latent CWE-476), and inherits the cryptographic weakness of legacy schemes (CWE-916).
The decisive full-length-equality gate that prevents the salt-only comparison bypass; central to the function's correctness.
Comparison correctness and side-channel resistance depend on slapi_ct_memcmp being truly constant-time and returning 0 only on full equality (latent contract, CWE-208 if violated).
Fail-closed error path; sets deny result on every non-matching branch. No security contract to violate.
Returns the computed deny/allow result; semantics are correct (0 == match) and fail-closed by construction.

findings.title: Authentication correctness depends on the paired length gates that prevent a salt-only comparison bypass
findings.anchor: if (dbpwd_len >= 3) { ... } and if (cp && dbpwd_len == strlen(cp)) { rc = slapi_ct_memcmp(dbpwd, cp, dbpwd_len); }
findings.bug_class: Authentication bypass via salt-only comparison (improper access control)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0031_cwe284.c (the guard logic) plus implementation of crypt_r/slapi_ct_memcmp (file not provided)
findings.invariant: The memcmp must always span the full length of a legitimately computed hash, and a stored value that is only salt (or otherwise shorter than a real hash) must never be treated as comparable. Both the dbpwd_len >= 3 pre-hash guard AND the dbpwd_len == strlen(cp) full-length equality must be present and unbypassable.
findings.failure_mode: crypt(3) echoes the salt as the leading bytes of its output. If the full-length equality (or the >= 3 guard) is removed/weakened, a stored value consisting of only the 2 salt characters causes crypt_r to reproduce those exact bytes at the front of cp for ANY userpwd; a comparison over the short stored length then matches unconditionally, allowing any client to bind as the victim. This is exactly the condition the code comment on lines 11-12 describes ('allow any password to bind as we then only compare SALTS'); the shown 'fixed' code correctly upholds the invariant, so the finding is that this contract is load-bearing and must not regress.
findings.what_to_confirm: That both guards remain in the shipped code and cannot be bypassed, that slapi_ct_memcmp returns 0 only on full-length equality, and that all callers interpret rc == 0 (not merely 'not -1') as the sole success condition.

findings.title: No NULL check on user-supplied password before crypt_r()
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (crypt_r, file not provided); missing guard is at corpus/eval/eval_0031_cwe284.c line 16
findings.invariant: userpwd must be a valid non-NULL C string on entry, or the function must reject NULL explicitly before calling crypt_r.
findings.failure_mode: crypt_r() dereferences its key argument. If a bind path can deliver a NULL (rather than empty-string) userpwd — e.g. a malformed or anonymous bind — crypt_r crashes the process, a remotely reachable denial of service. userpwd is attacker-controlled, so reachability depends only on whether any caller forwards NULL.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether every caller guarantees userpwd != NULL; if not, add an explicit NULL/empty check that returns -1 before line 16.

findings.title: Unconditional strlen(dbpwd) with no NULL check on the stored value
findings.invariant: dbpwd must be a valid non-NULL C string; malformed directory data must not reach strlen as NULL.
findings.failure_mode: strlen is the first operation and runs before any validation; a NULL stored password value dereferences NULL and crashes. Unlike the auth logic, this path is not fail-closed — it faults rather than returning deny. Reachability depends on whether the caller can pass a NULL dbpwd for an entry with a missing/empty {CRYPT} value.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the caller can ever pass dbpwd == NULL; if so, add a NULL guard returning -1 at the top of the function.

findings.title: Comparison side-channel safety is delegated to slapi_ct_memcmp's constant-time contract
findings.bug_class: Observable timing discrepancy in password comparison
findings.invariant: slapi_ct_memcmp must run in time independent of how many leading bytes match and must return 0 only on full-length byte equality.
findings.failure_mode: If the helper short-circuits on the first mismatching byte (like memcmp) or can return 0 for non-equal inputs, the auth path leaks match-prefix length via timing (a hash-comparison oracle) or accepts a non-matching hash. The caller-side usage here is correct (full length, correct operands), so the risk lives entirely in the helper's implementation.
findings.what_to_confirm: That slapi_ct_memcmp is a genuinely constant-time comparison returning 0 iff all dbpwd_len bytes are equal.

findings.title: Cryptographic strength inherited from the stored scheme (weak legacy DES / password truncation)
findings.bug_class: Use of password hash with insufficient computational effort
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0031_cwe284.c line 16 (scheme determined by the stored dbpwd value)
findings.invariant: Stored {CRYPT} values should use a strong scheme (e.g. $6$/SHA-512 with adequate rounds); the verifier should not silently honor weak legacy DES hashes.
findings.failure_mode: crypt_r faithfully re-hashes under whatever scheme dbpwd encodes. If dbpwd is a legacy 2-char-salt DES hash, userpwd is truncated to 8 bytes with a 12-bit salt, making stored credentials trivially brute-forceable. This function does not detect or reject weak schemes; it only compares. crypt_r may also return NULL or a '*'-prefixed error string for unsupported settings — both are safely rejected by the cp && / length-equality checks.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the directory still stores DES {CRYPT} values and whether policy should reject or migrate them to a modern scheme.

audit_checklist: Confirm both length gates are present and intact in the shipped code: dbpwd_len >= 3 before crypt_r (line 14) and dbpwd_len == strlen(cp) before the compare (line 19); verify neither can be bypassed, since together they prevent the salt-only authentication bypass.
Confirm slapi_ct_memcmp is genuinely constant-time and returns 0 only on full-length byte equality (no early short-circuit, no accidental 0 for unequal inputs).
Confirm every caller guarantees userpwd and dbpwd are non-NULL; otherwise add explicit NULL guards returning -1 before strlen (line 6) and crypt_r (line 16).
Confirm callers treat rc == 0 as the sole success condition (not merely rc != -1), matching slapi_ct_memcmp's 0-on-equal semantics.
Confirm struct crypt_data's only required pre-set field is .initialized (set to 0 here) and that its ~128 KB stack footprint is acceptable in the call context.
Confirm cp lifetime: it points into the stack-local data buffer and is used only before return; no reference to cp escapes the function.
Confirm the directory does not rely on weak legacy DES {CRYPT} values (8-byte truncation, 12-bit salt); prefer modern $6$/yescrypt schemes.
